title: FAA proposal: Supersonic airliners can fly over US cities if they’re quiet
gist: The FAA proposes overturning its 53-year ban on overland supersonic flight with an interim noise-based standard (sonic boom overpressure below 0.11 psf) modeled on Boom Supersonic's XB-1 demonstrations, aiming to finalize by mid-2027—though critics call the metric weak and Boom's own airliner faces 50/50 odds.

insights.claim: The FAA's June 30, 2026 proposed rule would replace the 1973 overland supersonic ban with an interim noise-based standard requiring sonic boom overpressure at the surface below 0.11 pounds per square foot.
insights.speaker: Jeremy Hsu
insights.so_what: A concrete regulatory threshold (0.11 psf) that defines the design target for any overland supersonic airliner—key gating metric for the sector.
insights.locator: The newly proposed rule would replace

insights.claim: The 0.11 psf standard is based on Boom Supersonic demonstrating quiet Mach cutoff flights with its XB-1, flying just beyond supersonic at high altitude so shockwaves refract upward rather than reaching the ground.
insights.speaker: Jeremy Hsu
insights.so_what: Regulation is being shaped around Boom's demonstrated capability, giving the startup a first-mover regulatory advantage.
insights.locator: That proposed standard is based on the Colorado-based startup

insights.claim: For comparison, the retired Concorde created sonic boom overpressure of 1.94 psf at Mach 2, roughly 18x the proposed 0.11 psf limit, illustrating how demanding the new standard is.
insights.speaker: Jeremy Hsu
insights.so_what: Quantifies the engineering gap between legacy supersonic and the new quiet standard—only novel airframes can qualify.
insights.locator: For comparison, the Concorde supersonic airliner

insights.claim: The FAA rule follows a June 6, 2025 Trump executive order, and Congress is advancing the Supersonic Aviation Modernization Act, which passed the House on March 24, 2026 and awaits a Senate vote.
insights.speaker: Jeremy Hsu
insights.so_what: Aligned executive, regulatory, and legislative momentum raises the probability of overland supersonic legalization.
insights.locator: US lawmakers in Congress have also been pushing

insights.claim: ICCT's Dan Rutherford called the overpressure metric weak, noting UN experts discarded it in 2014 because it doesn't actually measure loudness or annoyance.
insights.speaker: Jeremy Hsu
insights.so_what: Metric legitimacy dispute is a litigation/revision risk that could delay or reshape final rules before mid-2027.
insights.quote: I’m honestly surprised that the FAA would propose a rule this weak

insights.claim: NASA is testing a different approach with the Lockheed Martin X-59 Quesst, targeting sonic 'thumps' around 75 PldB—like a car door slamming 20 feet away—using perceived-decibel metrics rather than overpressure.
insights.speaker: Jeremy Hsu
insights.so_what: A rival perceived-loudness metric (PldB) from NASA/Lockheed could inform or supersede the FAA's overpressure standard.
insights.locator: Meanwhile, NASA has been testing a different approach

insights.claim: Boom Supersonic aims to deliver its Overture airliner to customers by 2029 and holds option agreements with American Airlines, Japan Airlines, and United Airlines.
insights.speaker: Jeremy Hsu
insights.so_what: Named airline options and a 2029 target define the commercial timeline and offtake demand to track for the supersonic thesis.
insights.locator: Boom Supersonic is developing a supersonic airliner

insights.claim: Boom has pivoted to producing natural gas turbines to power AI data centers, with CEO Blake Scholl saying that side-venture revenue would help fund Overture development.
insights.speaker: Jeremy Hsu
insights.so_what: A supersonic startup funding itself via AI-data-center gas turbines signals both cash-flow strain and the pull of AI-power demand.
insights.locator: But Boom has also pivoted away from its main goal

insights.claim: United Airlines CEO Scott Kirby gives Boom a '50/50' chance of getting its supersonic airliner flying, and legalization alone does not guarantee a commercial comeback given Concorde's unprofitable fuel economics and $2.8 billion development cost.
insights.speaker: Jeremy Hsu
insights.so_what: A key customer publicly pricing execution risk at coin-flip odds tempers the supersonic-revival investment case.
insights.locator: At the same time, United Airlines CEO Scott Kirby
